local_names.japanese: インスタントラーメン (乾麺)

aliases: インスタントラーメン (乾麺)

japanese: インスタントラーメン (乾麺)

region_of_origin: Japan (commercialised globally; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept identified for dry instant noodles as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

preparation_method: dry block — requires boiling before consumption

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dry instant noodle block weighs approximately 85 g; when prepared with the seasoning packet, total sodium per serving typically exceeds 1500 mg.

nutrient_source.title: Instant Noodles (Dry) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for instant noodles (dry), cr_id F05060066, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 (instant noodles, dry) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 for instant noodles (dry, boiled) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI values of 47–52 for instant noodles. The dry-block form is the uncooked state of the same product; GI applies to the prepared (boiled) form. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 22.1) / 100 ≈ 11.5 for an 85 g serving (22.1 g available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (517 mg/100 g in the dry block alone; total sodium with seasoning packet typically exceeds 1500 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit instant noodles; if consumed, use no more than half the seasoning packet and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or bean sprouts to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat carbohydrates (26 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.87 g/100 g) and GI of 52; palm-oil-derived saturated fat (≈4.2 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a block (≈42 g dry), always pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and consider lower-GI noodle alternatives such as soba or shirataki noodles for regular meals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (517 mg/100 g dry; >1500 mg/serving prepared), saturated fat from palm oil (≈4.2 g/100 g), and trace trans fat (≈0.1 g/100 g) presents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat instant noodles as an occasional food only; the high sodium elevates blood pressure, saturated fat raises LDL cholesterol, and even trace trans fat is associated with increased cardiovascular event risk — regular consumption is strongly discouraged.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Palm-oil deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trace trans fat; saturated fat and refined carbohydrates promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid instant noodles due to the pro-inflammatory combination of AGEs from palm-oil frying, trace trans fat, high saturated fat, and minimal anti-inflammatory nutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Instant noodles (dry) are ultra-processed wheat-flour noodle blocks that have been pre-cooked by deep-frying in palm oil and then dehydrated, producing a shelf-stable dry brick that reconstitutes rapidly in boiling water. The product is sold with a separate seasoning sachet containing salt, flavour enhancers, and spices. Per 100 g of the dry block, the food provides 206 kcal, 5.18 g protein, 9.01 g fat (of which approximately 4.2 g is saturated fat from palm oil and a trace of trans fat at ~0.1 g), and 26 g carbohydrate with only 0.87 g dietary fibre and 3.62 g sugar. Sodium in the dry block alone is 517 mg/100 g; when the seasoning sachet is added during preparation, total sodium per 85 g serving routinely exceeds 1500 mg — well above the recommended 400 mg per meal for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index of instant noodles is approximately 52 (low GI range), but the low fibre content and refined carbohydrate matrix mean postprandial glucose response is still clinically significant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The product is widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a quick meal or late-night snack. From a clinical nutrition standpoint, instant noodles are a high-sodium, high-saturated-fat, low-fibre ultra-processed food that poses meaningful risk for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and inflammatory conditions when consumed regularly. Iron content is notable at 2.01 mg/100 g, likely from fortification. Calcium is low at 12 mg/100 g. The food is not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diets without label verification, as seasoning packets frequently contain animal-derived flavour compounds.
common_user_queries: Are instant noodles bad for you?
How much sodium is in instant noodles?
Is instant noodle GI high or low?
Are instant noodles high in saturated fat?
Can I eat instant noodles if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic load of instant noodles?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060066, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 (instant noodles, dry) as a close compositional analogue; these secondary values are clearly documented. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (3.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 (instant noodles, dry) because HPB Singapore Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. These values are consistent with the known palm-oil frying process and should be verified against product label data when available. 2. Potassium (120 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 — plant-oil-fried wheat product contains no cholesterol). 4. Fields set to -1 (data not available in approved sources): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central NDB 18069 at the level of individual vitamins and minerals beyond calcium and iron; follow-up with product label data is recommended. 5. GI of 52 applies to the boiled/prepared form; the dry block is the uncooked state of the same product. Glycemic load of 11.5 calculated for an 85 g dry serving (≈22.1 g available carbohydrate). 6. Halal and vegetarian status are not confirmed — seasoning packets vary by brand and may contain pork-derived or animal-derived flavour compounds; label verification is required. 7. Cultural classification: although the input record listed 'western' as primary cuisine, instant noodles were invented in Japan (Nissin, 1958) and are most widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia; cuisine has been corrected to 'Pan-Asian / Global'.
